items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher; secondary data already exists and was produced by others.
items.misconception: Primary data is always more valuable than secondary data.
items.random_wrong: Primary data means the first page of a report.
items.why: Students rank the two types rather than distinguishing them by who collected the data and when.

items.q: Why do positivists prefer quantitative methods?
items.correct: Because they seek objective, measurable patterns and law-like generalisations about society.
items.misconception: Because numbers are simply easier to collect than words.
items.random_wrong: Because they reject all forms of observation.
items.why: Learners reduce the preference to convenience rather than its aim of scientific, objective measurement.

items.q: What is triangulation in social research?
items.correct: Using more than one method or source to check and strengthen findings.
items.misconception: Always using exactly three different researchers.
items.why: The 'tri' prefix misleads students into thinking it requires three of something rather than multiple methods.

items.q: Weber's concept for empathetic understanding of social action is:
items.why: It's Verstehen. “Anomie” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which research method involves in-depth participant observation?
items.why: It's Ethnography. “Survey” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Durkheim publish 'Suicide'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1897 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
